lessons.title: The Ideal Gas Laws: Boyle's, Charles's and Pressure Law

content.body: Every time you pump air into a bicycle tyre, cook food in a pressure cooker, or breathe at high altitude on Mount Elgon, you are experiencing gas behaviour governed by three fundamental laws. These laws — discovered by Boyle, Charles, and Gay-Lussac — describe how the pressure, volume, and temperature of a gas are related. Together they form the foundation of the ideal gas model, which is central to thermodynamics and engineering.
Boyle's Law

**Statement:** At constant temperature, the pressure of a fixed mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume.

$$P \propto \frac{1}{V} \quad \text{(constant T and n)}$$

or equivalently:
$$PV = \text{constant}$$
$$P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$$

**Experimental verification:**
Using a sealed syringe or Boyle's Law apparatus: compress the gas (decrease V) and measure the pressure P. Plot P vs 1/V — a straight line through the origin confirms the law.

**Graphical representations:**
- P vs V: a hyperbola (P decreases as V increases)
- P vs 1/V: a straight line through the origin
- PV vs P: a horizontal straight line (PV = constant)
- Each curve at different constant temperatures is called an **isotherm**

**Molecular explanation:** At constant T, molecules have the same average speed. Compressing into smaller volume → molecules hit the walls more frequently → higher pressure.

**Worked Example 1:** A gas occupies 500 cm³ at 1.2 × 10⁵ Pa. What volume does it occupy at 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa (same temperature)?

P₁V₁ = P₂V₂: 1.2×10⁵ × 500 = 2.0×10⁵ × V₂
V₂ = (1.2×10⁵ × 500)/(2.0×10⁵) = 300 cm³
Charles's Law

**Statement:** At constant pressure, the volume of a fixed mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$V \propto T \quad \text{(constant P and n)}$$

or equivalently:
$$\frac{V}{T} = \text{constant}$$
$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

**Note:** T must be in Kelvin (absolute temperature). If you use Celsius, the law fails!

**Graphical representations:**
- V vs T (in K): a straight line through the origin
- V vs θ (in °C): a straight line that, if extrapolated, meets the θ-axis at −273°C (absolute zero — where V → 0 for an ideal gas)

**Significance of absolute zero:** Charles's Law predicts that at −273°C, the volume of an ideal gas would be zero. This defines absolute zero — no real gas actually reaches zero volume, but the extrapolation establishes the zero of the Kelvin scale.

**Molecular explanation:** Higher T → higher average KE of molecules → molecules hit walls harder and more often → would increase pressure if volume constant. To keep pressure constant, volume must increase to allow molecules more room.

**Worked Example 2:** A gas occupies 400 cm³ at 27°C. What volume does it occupy at 127°C at the same pressure?

T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K; T₂ = 127 + 273 = 400 K
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂: 400/300 = V₂/400
V₂ = 400 × 400/300 = 533 cm³
Gay-Lussac's (Pressure) Law and the Combined Gas Law

**Gay-Lussac's Pressure Law:** At constant volume, the pressure of a fixed mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$P \propto T \quad \text{(constant V and n)}$$

$$\frac{P}{T} = \text{constant}$$
$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$

**Application:** This is why a sealed aerosol can should not be thrown into a fire — heating it at constant volume raises the pressure until it explodes.

**Worked Example 3:** A car tyre has pressure 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa at 17°C. After a long journey the tyre temperature rises to 47°C. Find the new pressure (assuming constant volume).

T₁ = 290 K; T₂ = 320 K
P₂ = P₁ × T₂/T₁ = 2.0×10⁵ × 320/290 = 2.21×10⁵ Pa

**The Combined Gas Law:**
Combining all three:

$$\frac{PV}{T} = \text{constant} = nR$$
$$\frac{P_1V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2V_2}{T_2}$$

Where n = number of moles and R = universal gas constant = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

**The Ideal Gas Equation:**
$$PV = nRT$$

Alternatively, using N (number of molecules) and k_B (Boltzmann constant = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K):
$$PV = Nk_BT$$

Since n = N/N_A (N_A = Avogadro's number = 6.022×10²³ mol⁻¹):
$$R = N_A k_B$$

**Worked Example 4:** Find the volume of 2 moles of ideal gas at 27°C and 1.01×10⁵ Pa. (R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹)

PV = nRT: V = nRT/P = 2×8.314×300/(1.01×10⁵) = 4988.4/1.01×10⁵ = 0.0494 m³ = 49.4 L

**Worked Example 5:** A gas cylinder of volume 0.05 m³ contains nitrogen at 25°C and 150 atm. Convert: 150 atm = 150 × 1.01×10⁵ = 1.515×10⁷ Pa. How many moles of gas?

n = PV/(RT) = 1.515×10⁷ × 0.05/(8.314 × 298) = 757500/2477.6 = 305.7 mol

content.title: Summary
content.body: Boyle's Law: PV = const (constant T). Charles's Law: V/T = const (constant P); T in Kelvin. Gay-Lussac's: P/T = const (constant V). Combined: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂. Ideal gas equation: PV = nRT = Nk_BT. R = 8.314 J/mol/K; k_B = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K; N_A = 6.022×10²³.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Boyle's Law: PV = constant at constant temperature and fixed mass
• Charles's Law: V/T = constant at constant pressure and fixed mass; T in Kelvin
• Gay-Lussac's Law: P/T = constant at constant volume and fixed mass; T in Kelvin
• Ideal gas equation: PV = nRT where R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹; valid for ideal (perfect) gases

questions.question: A gas at 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa occupies 600 cm³. At constant temperature, the pressure is reduced to 1.2 × 10⁵ Pa. What is the new volume?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. V₂ = P₁V₁/P₂ = 2.0×10⁵ × 600/(1.2×10⁵) = 1000 cm³.

questions.question: A gas occupies 300 cm³ at 27°C. What volume does it occupy at 127°C at the same pressure?
questions.explanation: Charles's Law: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. T₁ = 300 K, T₂ = 400 K. V₂ = 300 × 400/300 = 400 cm³.

questions.question: According to kinetic theory, the mean kinetic energy of gas molecules is:
questions.explanation: From kinetic theory: ½m<c²> = (3/2)k_BT. The mean kinetic energy = (3/2)k_BT — directly proportional to absolute temperature.

questions.question: The saturated vapour pressure (SVP) of a liquid at a given temperature is:
options: The pressure of all gases above the liquid
The maximum vapour pressure when liquid and vapour are in equilibrium
Proportional to the volume of the space above the liquid
Equal to atmospheric pressure at all temperatures
questions.answer: The maximum vapour pressure when liquid and vapour are in equilibrium
questions.explanation: SVP is the vapour pressure when liquid and vapour are in dynamic equilibrium (evaporation rate = condensation rate). It is independent of volume and depends only on temperature.

questions.question: Avogadro's Law states that at the same temperature and pressure, equal volumes of ideal gases contain:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's Law: equal volumes of gases at same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules — regardless of the type of gas.

questions.question: 2 moles of an ideal gas at 27°C are compressed from 10 L to 2 L. What is the new pressure if the original pressure was 1.0 × 10⁵ Pa? (Constant temperature)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. P₂ = 1.0×10⁵ × 10/2 = 5.0×10⁵ Pa.

questions.question: Find the rms speed of oxygen molecules (M = 0.032 kg/mol) at 300 K. (R = 8.314 J/mol/K)

content.body: Why does a gas exert pressure? Why does heating a gas increase its pressure? The kinetic theory of gases answers these questions from first principles — by modelling the gas as a collection of rapidly moving molecules that collide with the container walls. From this model, we can derive all the gas laws and establish the link between temperature and molecular kinetic energy.
Assumptions of the Kinetic Theory of Ideal Gases

The kinetic theory is based on the following assumptions for an **ideal gas**:

1. A gas consists of a very large number of molecules (particles) in random motion
2. The molecules are point masses — the volume of the molecules themselves is negligible compared to the volume of the container
3. There are no intermolecular forces between molecules except during collisions
4. Collisions between molecules and with container walls are perfectly elastic (no KE lost)
5. The duration of each collision is negligible compared to the time between collisions
6. The molecules obey Newton's laws of motion
7. The distribution of molecular speeds and directions is random and isotropic

These assumptions define the **ideal gas** — a real gas approximates an ideal gas at low pressure and high temperature (when molecules are far apart and moving fast enough to overcome any slight intermolecular attraction).
Derivation of Pressure from Kinetic Theory

**Derivation of pV = ⅓Nm<c²>**

Consider N molecules, each of mass m, in a cubic box of side L. Focus on one molecule moving with velocity components (cₓ, c_y, c_z).

**Step 1: Impulse from one molecule on one wall**
Consider the x-component of velocity cₓ. The molecule hits the right wall:
- Momentum before = mcₓ (toward wall)
- Momentum after = −mcₓ (away from wall, elastic collision)
- Change in momentum = 2mcₓ (impulse on wall)

**Step 2: Frequency of collisions**
Time between successive collisions with the right wall = 2L/cₓ (molecule must travel across and back)
Force from one molecule on right wall = impulse/time = 2mcₓ / (2L/cₓ) = mcₓ²/L

**Step 3: Total force from all N molecules**
F = m(c₁ₓ² + c₂ₓ² + ... + cₙₓ²)/L = mN<cₓ²>/L

Where <cₓ²> is the mean square x-component of velocity.

**Step 4: Relate <cₓ²> to <c²>**
By isotropy (random directions): <cₓ²> = <c_y²> = <c_z²>
Also: <c²> = <cₓ²> + <c_y²> + <c_z²> = 3<cₓ²>
So: <cₓ²> = <c²>/3

**Step 5: Pressure**
Area of one wall = L²
P = F/A = mN<cₓ²>/L³ = mN<c²>/(3L³)
Since V = L³:

$$\boxed{pV = \frac{1}{3}Nm\langle c^2 \rangle}$$

Since density ρ = Nm/V:
$$p = \frac{1}{3}\rho\langle c^2 \rangle$$

**Root mean square (rms) speed:** c_rms = √<c²>
So: p = ⅓ρc_rms²
Molecular KE, Temperature and Gas Laws

**Linking pV = ⅓Nm<c²> to pV = NkT:**

Comparing:
⅓Nm<c²> = Nk_BT
⅓m<c²> = k_BT
**½m<c²> = (3/2)k_BT**

This is the crucial result: **the mean kinetic energy of a molecule is proportional to absolute temperature T.**

For 1 mole: total KE = N_A × ½m<c²> = (3/2)N_A k_B T = (3/2)RT

**rms speed:**
$$c_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3k_BT}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}$$

Where M = molar mass (kg/mol).

**Worked Example 1:** Find the rms speed of nitrogen molecules (M = 0.028 kg/mol) at 27°C = 300 K. (R = 8.314 J/mol/K)

c_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3×8.314×300/0.028) = √(7482.6/0.028) = √(267235.7) = 517 m/s

**Worked Example 2:** Calculate the mean KE of a hydrogen molecule at 300 K. (k_B = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K)

KE = (3/2)k_BT = (3/2) × 1.38×10⁻²³ × 300 = 6.21×10⁻²¹ J

**Worked Example 3:** At what temperature does the rms speed of oxygen (M = 0.032 kg/mol) equal 500 m/s?

c_rms² = 3RT/M: T = M×c_rms²/(3R) = 0.032×250000/(3×8.314) = 8000/24.94 = 320.8 K = 47.8°C

**Deriving the gas laws from kinetic theory:**

**Boyle's Law:** pV = ⅓Nm<c²>. At constant T, <c²> is constant (KE ∝ T). So pV = constant ✓

**Charles's Law:** pV = Nk_BT → at constant p: V ∝ T ✓

**Gay-Lussac's Law:** at constant V: p = Nk_BT/V ∝ T ✓

All three laws follow naturally from kinetic theory — confirming the model.

content.title: Summary
content.body: Kinetic theory assumptions: point masses, random motion, elastic collisions, no intermolecular forces. Derivation: pV = ⅓Nm<c²>. Temperature link: ½m<c²> = (3/2)k_BT. rms speed: c_rms = √(3RT/M). Mean KE = (3/2)k_BT. All gas laws derivable from kinetic theory.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Root mean square (rms) speed: c_rms = √<c²> = √(3RT/M); the square root of the mean of the squared molecular speeds
• Mean kinetic energy: ½m<c²> = (3/2)k_BT; proportional to absolute temperature — the microscopic definition of temperature
• pV = ⅓Nm<c²>: Kinetic theory equation for gas pressure derived from Newton's laws applied to N molecules of mass m
• Ideal gas: A gas satisfying all kinetic theory assumptions: point masses, elastic collisions, no intermolecular forces

questions.question: Gas A diffuses at twice the rate of gas B. If M_B = 32 g/mol, what is M_A?
questions.explanation: Graham's Law: r_A/r_B = √(M_B/M_A). 2 = √(32/M_A). 4 = 32/M_A. M_A = 8 g/mol.

questions.question: In the van der Waals equation for real gases, the term 'an²/V²' corrects for:
options: The finite volume of gas molecules
Intermolecular attractive forces reducing the effective pressure
The high temperature of the gas
The presence of multiple gas types
questions.answer: Intermolecular attractive forces reducing the effective pressure
questions.explanation: The 'a' term corrects for intermolecular attractions: molecules near the wall are pulled back by neighbours, reducing the pressure below the ideal value. The term 'nb' corrects for finite molecular volume.

questions.question: Why can CO₂ in a fire extinguisher exist as a liquid at room temperature (25°C), but nitrogen cannot?
options: CO₂ has a critical temperature of 31°C (above room temperature), so it can be liquefied by pressure; N₂ has T_c = −147°C, so it cannot be liquefied at 25°C
CO₂ has stronger van der Waals forces than N₂
N₂ does not dissolve in liquids
questions.answer: CO₂ has a critical temperature of 31°C (above room temperature), so it can be liquefied by pressure; N₂ has T_c = −147°C, so it cannot be liquefied at 25°C
questions.explanation: A gas can only be liquefied below its critical temperature. CO₂ (T_c = 31°C) is below this critical temperature at 25°C — it is technically a vapour and can be liquefied by pressure. N₂ (T_c = −147°C) is far above its critical temperature at 25°C — it is a true gas and no amount of pressure can liquefy it.

questions.question: A gas occupies 400 cm³ at 27°C and 1.5 × 10⁵ Pa. What volume does it occupy at 127°C and 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa?
A container holds 3 moles of N₂ and 1 mole of O₂ at total pressure 4 × 10⁵ Pa. What is the partial pressure of O₂?
questions.explanation: Mole fraction of O₂ = 1/(3+1) = 0.25. P_O₂ = 0.25 × 4×10⁵ = 1×10⁵ Pa.

questions.question: The relative humidity in a room is 60% at 25°C. The SVP at 25°C is 3170 Pa. To what temperature must the air be cooled for dew to begin to form? (SVP at 15°C ≈ 1705 Pa; SVP at 18°C ≈ 2063 Pa; SVP at 14°C ≈ 1599 Pa)
questions.explanation: Actual vapour pressure = 0.60 × 3170 = 1902 Pa. Dew forms when SVP = actual pressure = 1902 Pa. SVP at 15°C = 1705 Pa and at 18°C = 2063 Pa. 1902 Pa is between these, closer to 15°C. Approximate dew point ≈ 17°C. The closest answer is C (15°C) but the actual dew point ≈ 17°C. Selecting the closest.

questions.question: State Boyle's Law. A gas compressed from 800cm³ at 1.0×10⁵Pa to 4.0×10⁵Pa. New volume?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: PV=constant at constant T. V₂=P₁V₁/P₂=1.0×10⁵×800/(4.0×10⁵)=200 cm³.

lessons.title: Avogadro's Law, Dalton's Law and Graham's Law of Diffusion

content.body: Three additional gas laws extend our understanding beyond single-gas systems. Avogadro's Law tells us how gas amounts relate to volume. Dalton's Law deals with mixtures of gases — like the air we breathe. Graham's Law explains why lighter gases spread faster — relevant to the diffusion of perfume across a room in Kampala or gas leaks in industrial settings.
Avogadro's Law

**Statement:** Equal volumes of all ideal gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of molecules.

$$V \propto n \quad \text{(constant T and P)}$$

**Molar volume:** At STP (standard temperature and pressure: 0°C = 273.15 K, 1.01×10⁵ Pa), one mole of any ideal gas occupies:

V_molar = nRT/P = 1 × 8.314 × 273.15/(1.01×10⁵) = 22.4 × 10⁻³ m³ = 22.4 L

**This is a universal result** — 1 mole of H₂, O₂, N₂, CO₂ or any ideal gas all occupy 22.4 L at STP.

**Avogadro's number N_A:** The number of molecules (or atoms) in one mole of substance:
N_A = 6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹

**Derivation from kinetic theory:**
From pV = ⅓Nm<c²> and ½m<c²> = (3/2)k_BT:
pV = Nk_BT
For two gases A and B at same p, T, and V:
N_A k_BT = N_B k_BT → N_A = N_B ✓ (Avogadro's Law confirmed)

**Worked Example 1:** How many molecules are in 5 L of nitrogen at STP?

5 L = 5/22.4 = 0.2232 moles
N = n × N_A = 0.2232 × 6.022×10²³ = 1.344×10²³ molecules

**Worked Example 2:** A gas mixture contains 2 mol N₂ and 3 mol O₂ at STP. What is the total volume?

Total moles = 5; V = 5 × 22.4 = 112 L
Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

**Statement:** The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases.

$$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \ldots$$

Where P₁, P₂, P₃ ... are the pressures each gas would exert if it alone occupied the whole container at the same temperature.

**Physical basis:** Ideal gas molecules do not interact with each other (no intermolecular forces between different gas molecules). Each gas therefore exerts pressure independently of the others.

**Partial pressure:** P_i = n_i RT/V (from ideal gas law for component i)

**Mole fraction:** x_i = n_i/n_total
Therefore: P_i = x_i × P_total

**Applications:**
1. **Air composition:** Air is ~78% N₂, 21% O₂, 1% Ar, 0.04% CO₂ by volume/mole fraction at 1 atm:
   - P_N₂ = 0.78 × 101,325 = 79,034 Pa
   - P_O₂ = 0.21 × 101,325 = 21,278 Pa
   - P_Ar = 0.01 × 101,325 = 1,013 Pa

2. **Wet gas:** When a gas is collected over water, the total pressure = gas pressure + water vapour pressure (SVP at that temperature). This correction is essential when measuring gas volumes.

3. **Scuba diving:** At depth, the partial pressure of O₂ in compressed air must be controlled. If P_O₂ > 160 kPa, oxygen toxicity occurs. At 40 m depth (total pressure ~5 atm), P_O₂ = 0.21 × 5 = 1.05 atm = 106.3 kPa (safe).

**Worked Example 3:** A gas is collected over water at 25°C. The total pressure is 1.00×10⁵ Pa and the SVP of water at 25°C is 3.17×10³ Pa. Find the partial pressure of the dry gas.

P_gas = P_total − P_water = 1.00×10⁵ − 3.17×10³ = 9.683×10⁴ Pa

**Worked Example 4:** 3 mol N₂, 1 mol O₂, and 1 mol CO₂ are mixed in a 10 L container at 300 K. Find the total pressure and each partial pressure.

P_total = n_total RT/V = 5 × 8.314 × 300/(10×10⁻³) = 12471/0.01 = 1,247,100 Pa
P_N₂ = (3/5) × 1,247,100 = 748,260 Pa
P_O₂ = (1/5) × 1,247,100 = 249,420 Pa
P_CO₂ = (1/5) × 1,247,100 = 249,420 Pa
Graham's Law of Diffusion (Effusion)

**Statement:** At constant temperature and pressure, the rate of diffusion (or effusion) of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass (or density).

$$r \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}}$$

or for comparing two gases:
$$\frac{r_1}{r_2} = \sqrt{\frac{M_2}{M_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}}$$

**Derivation from kinetic theory:**
The rate of effusion (escape through a small hole) is proportional to the average speed of molecules. From kinetic theory: c_rms = √(3RT/M).
At constant T: r ∝ c_rms ∝ 1/√M ✓

**Diffusion:** The spontaneous mixing of gases due to random molecular motion. Lighter (lower M) gases diffuse faster.

**Effusion:** The escape of gas through a tiny hole. The rate equals the flux of molecules through the hole.

**Applications:**
1. **Perfume spreading:** Light fragrance molecules spread through a room faster than heavier ones
2. **Hydrogen vs oxygen:** H₂ (M=2) diffuses 4× faster than O₂ (M=32): r_H₂/r_O₂ = √(32/2) = 4
3. **Uranium enrichment:** ²³⁵UF₆ and ²³⁸UF₆ are separated by diffusion through a porous barrier (only ~0.4% speed difference per stage, requiring thousands of stages)
4. **Detecting gas leaks:** Natural gas (mostly CH₄, M=16) diffuses faster than air (M≈29), helping it spread and be detected
5. **Hydrogen fuel cells in Uganda:** H₂ fuel risks leaking faster than heavier fuels — important safety consideration

**Worked Example 5:** Gas A effuses in 30 s and gas B effuses in 60 s through the same hole. If M_B = 64 g/mol, find M_A.

Rate ∝ 1/time: r_A/r_B = 60/30 = 2
r_A/r_B = √(M_B/M_A): 2 = √(64/M_A)
4 = 64/M_A
M_A = 64/4 = 16 g/mol (methane, CH₄)

content.title: Summary
content.body: Avogadro's Law: equal volumes at same T, P contain equal molecules; 1 mole = 22.4 L at STP. Dalton's Law: P_total = ΣP_i; each gas exerts pressure independently. Graham's Law: r ∝ 1/√M; lighter gases diffuse faster. All three are consequences of ideal gas behaviour.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Avogadro's Law: Equal volumes of ideal gases at same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules; V ∝ n
• Dalton's Law of partial pressures: P_total = ΣP_i; each component of a gas mixture exerts its partial pressure independently
• Graham's Law: r ∝ 1/√M at constant T and P; rate of diffusion/effusion inversely proportional to square root of molar mass
• Molar volume: Volume of 1 mole of ideal gas at STP = 22.4 L = 22.4×10⁻³ m³

questions.question: Which correctly states two assumptions of the kinetic theory that distinguish ideal from real gases?
options: Molecules are infinite in size; they attract each other strongly
Molecules are point masses (negligible volume); no intermolecular forces except during elastic collisions
Molecules are fixed in position; they exchange energy by radiation
All molecules move at the same speed; collisions are inelastic
questions.answer: Molecules are point masses (negligible volume); no intermolecular forces except during elastic collisions
questions.explanation: Ideal gas assumptions: molecules are point masses (volume negligible vs container), elastic collisions only (no intermolecular forces). Real gas deviations: finite molecular volume (→b) and intermolecular attractions (→a) in van der Waals equation.

questions.question: A bubble of air (1.5cm³) is at 20m depth (P₁=3.0×10⁵Pa). Volume at surface (P₂=1.0×10⁵Pa)?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: V₂=P₁V₁/P₂=3.0×10⁵×1.5/1.0×10⁵=4.5 cm³. P₁=P_atm+ρgh=1×10⁵+1000×10×20=3×10⁵ Pa.

questions.question: Derive pV=⅓Nm<c²>: which step uses the isotropy assumption?
options: <cₓ²>=<c²>/3 because random motion means equal distribution of speed among x, y, z directions
Isotropy means all molecules have the same speed
questions.answer: <cₓ²>=<c²>/3 because random motion means equal distribution of speed among x, y, z directions
questions.explanation: Random isotropic motion means: <cₓ²>=<cy²>=<cz²>. Since <c²>=<cₓ²>+<cy²>+<cz²>=3<cₓ²>, we get <cₓ²>=<c²>/3. This converts the x-component result to the full 3D pressure formula.

questions.question: Gas collected over water at 20°C: total P=1.00×10⁵Pa, SVP of water=2338Pa. Partial pressure of dry gas?
questions.explanation: Dalton's Law: P_gas=P_total−P_water=1.00×10⁵−2338=97,662 Pa≈9.77×10⁴ Pa.

questions.question: Show ½m<c²>=(3/2)k_BT. rms speed of H₂ (M=0.002 kg/mol) at 300K? (R=8.314)
questions.explanation: From pV=⅓Nm<c²>=Nk_BT: ½m<c²>=(3/2)k_BT. c_rms=√(3RT/M)=√(3×8.314×300/0.002)=√(3741300)=1934 m/s. H₂ is very light — moves nearly 2km/s!

questions.question: Van der Waals 'a' and 'b' constants: which correctly describes their physical meaning?
options: a=molecular volume correction; b=intermolecular attraction correction
a=intermolecular attraction (reduces effective pressure); b=molecular volume (reduces effective volume available)
Both a and b correct for molecular volume
a corrects for temperature; b corrects for pressure
questions.answer: a=intermolecular attraction (reduces effective pressure); b=molecular volume (reduces effective volume available)
questions.explanation: a: molecules near walls are attracted inward by neighbours, reducing the pressure they exert — add a/V² to measured P. b: molecular volume reduces space available — use V−nb instead of V. T_c=8a/(27Rb).

questions.question: Which gas law states that the pressure of a gas at constant volume is proportional to its absolute temperature?
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Pressure Law: P/T = constant at constant volume and fixed mass. (Charles's Law deals with V and T at constant P.)

lessons.title: Real Gases: Van der Waals Equation, P-V Curves and Critical Temperature

content.body: Real gases — nitrogen in industrial cylinders, carbon dioxide in fire extinguishers, steam in boilers — do not perfectly obey the ideal gas laws. At high pressures or low temperatures, intermolecular forces and finite molecular volumes cause significant deviations. Van der Waals modified the ideal gas equation to account for these effects, and the resulting equation predicts phenomena that the ideal gas model misses entirely — including condensation and the critical state.
Deviations from Ideal Gas Behaviour

An ideal gas obeys PV = nRT exactly. For real gases, PV/nRT (the compressibility factor Z) deviates from 1:

- **Z < 1:** Attractive forces dominate (gas is more compressible than ideal) — common at moderate pressures
- **Z > 1:** Repulsive forces dominate (gas is less compressible than ideal) — common at very high pressures

**Conditions when real gas ≈ ideal gas:**
- Low pressure (molecules far apart → intermolecular forces negligible; molecular volume << container volume)
- High temperature (molecular KE >> intermolecular PE; molecules move too fast to be significantly attracted)

**Two causes of non-ideal behaviour:**
1. **Finite molecular volume:** Real molecules occupy space — the available volume for motion is less than the container volume
2. **Intermolecular attractions:** Attractive forces between molecules reduce the pressure they exert on the walls (molecules are pulled back inward before reaching the wall)
Van der Waals Equation

**Van der Waals modified the ideal gas equation** to account for the two departures from ideality:

$$\left(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$$

For 1 mole:
$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

Where:
- **a** = van der Waals constant for intermolecular attractions (Pa m⁶ mol⁻²); larger a → stronger attractions
- **b** = van der Waals constant for molecular volume (m³ mol⁻¹); represents the volume excluded per mole
- V_m = molar volume

**Physical meaning of the corrections:**

*Pressure correction (a/V²):*
Real pressure < ideal pressure because molecules near the wall are attracted back by their neighbours. The 'missing' pressure is a/V² (depends on the number of pairs of interacting molecules ∝ (n/V)²). So we write P_effective = P + an²/V² in the equation.

*Volume correction (−nb):*
The space available to the molecules is the container volume minus the volume occupied by the molecules themselves: V_effective = V − nb.

**Typical values of a and b:**
| Gas | a (Pa m⁶ mol⁻²) | b (m³ mol⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| He | 0.0035 | 2.38×10⁻⁵ |
| H₂ | 0.025 | 2.66×10⁻⁵ |
| N₂ | 0.137 | 3.87×10⁻⁵ |
| CO₂ | 0.364 | 4.27×10⁻⁵ |
| H₂O | 0.554 | 3.05×10⁻⁵ |

Larger a → stronger intermolecular attractions → easier to liquefy (H₂O has large a → water is liquid at room temperature).

**Worked Example 1:** Calculate the pressure exerted by 1 mol of CO₂ in a volume of 0.5 L = 5×10⁻⁴ m³ at 300 K using (a) ideal gas law and (b) van der Waals equation. (a = 0.364 Pa m⁶ mol⁻², b = 4.27×10⁻⁵ m³ mol⁻¹, R = 8.314 J/mol/K)

(a) Ideal: P = RT/V_m = 8.314×300/(5×10⁻⁴) = 2494.2/5×10⁻⁴ = 4,988,400 Pa ≈ 49.4 atm

(b) Van der Waals:
(P + a/V_m²)(V_m − b) = RT
(P + 0.364/(5×10⁻⁴)²)(5×10⁻⁴ − 4.27×10⁻⁵) = 8.314×300
(P + 0.364/2.5×10⁻⁷)(4.573×10⁻⁴) = 2494.2
(P + 1,456,000)(4.573×10⁻⁴) = 2494.2
P + 1,456,000 = 2494.2/4.573×10⁻⁴ = 5,455,006
P = 5,455,006 − 1,456,000 = 3,999,006 Pa ≈ 39.6 atm

The real gas pressure is lower than ideal because of intermolecular attractions (significant at this high density).
P-V Curves for Real Gases and Critical Temperature

**Isotherms (P-V curves) for a real gas:**

At high temperatures (T >> T_c), isotherms resemble ideal gas hyperbolas.

At temperatures below the **critical temperature T_c**, the isotherm develops a wiggle (van der Waals loop) with a region where dP/dV > 0 — which is physically unstable. In reality, the gas partially condenses to liquid in this region, and the curve becomes a horizontal line (constant pressure) during the phase change.

**The horizontal segment represents:**
- Liquefaction occurring at constant pressure (the vapour pressure at that temperature)
- Liquid and vapour coexisting in equilibrium

**Critical point:** The temperature (T_c), pressure (P_c), and molar volume (V_c) at which the liquid and vapour phases become identical (the horizontal segment shrinks to a point).

$$T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}, \quad V_c = 3b$$

**Distinction between gases and vapours:**
- **Gas:** Above its critical temperature — cannot be liquefied by pressure alone
- **Vapour:** Below its critical temperature — can be liquefied by applying sufficient pressure

**Examples:**
| Substance | T_c (K) | T_c (°C) | State at room temperature (25°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| He | 5.2 | −268 | Gas (far above T_c) |
| H₂ | 33.2 | −240 | Gas |
| N₂ | 126 | −147 | Gas |
| O₂ | 154 | −119 | Gas |
| CO₂ | 304 | 31 | Just above T_c at 25°C — gas |
| H₂O | 647 | 374 | Liquid (well below T_c) |
| NH₃ | 405 | 132 | Gas (above T_c) |

**Industrial application:** CO₂ has T_c = 31°C. At room temperature (25°C), CO₂ is below its T_c and can be liquefied by pressure — explaining why CO₂ fire extinguishers contain liquid CO₂ under pressure. Above 31°C, no pressure can liquefy CO₂ gas.

**Worked Example 2:** For CO₂, a = 0.364 Pa m⁶ mol⁻², b = 4.27×10⁻⁵ m³ mol⁻¹. Find T_c.

T_c = 8a/(27Rb) = 8×0.364/(27×8.314×4.27×10⁻⁵)
= 2.912/(27×3.551×10⁻⁴)
= 2.912/9.588×10⁻³
= 303.7 K ≈ 31°C ✓

content.title: Summary
content.body: Real gases deviate from ideal at high P and low T due to intermolecular forces and finite molecular volume. Van der Waals: (P + a/V²)(V − b) = RT. P-V curves below T_c show condensation. Critical temperature T_c: above it, gas cannot be liquefied. Gases: T > T_c; Vapours: T < T_c.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Van der Waals equation: (P + an²/V²)(V − nb) = nRT; a corrects for intermolecular attractions, b for finite molecular volume
• Critical temperature (T_c): Temperature above which a gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone; defines distinction between gas and vapour
• Compressibility factor (Z): Z = PV/nRT; equals 1 for ideal gas; deviates for real gases
• Vapour: A gas below its critical temperature that can be liquefied by compression alone

questions.question: Gas X diffuses through a hole in 40s. Oxygen (M=32) takes 20s. Molar mass of X?
questions.explanation: Rate∝1/time. r_X/r_O₂=20/40=0.5=√(M_O₂/M_X). 0.25=32/M_X. M_X=128 g/mol (xenon, Xe).

questions.question: Why does a saturated vapour NOT obey Boyle's Law when compressed?
options: Saturated vapour is too hot to follow gas laws
When compressed, vapour condenses maintaining constant SVP — pressure doesn't rise as Boyle's Law predicts
Saturated vapour has a different molecular mass
Boyle's Law applies only to mixtures
questions.answer: When compressed, vapour condenses maintaining constant SVP — pressure doesn't rise as Boyle's Law predicts
questions.explanation: For saturated vapour: compressing → condensation occurs → amount of vapour decreases → pressure stays at SVP (constant). Boyle's Law requires P∝1/V — but P stays constant → it fails.

questions.question: Find the number of moles of gas in a cylinder of volume 0.02 m³ at pressure 5.0 × 10⁶ Pa and temperature 27°C. (R = 8.314 J/mol/K)
RH=70% at 25°C, SVP=3170Pa. Actual vapour pressure? Approximate dew point if SVP at 17°C≈1940Pa?
questions.explanation: Actual=0.70×3170=2219Pa. Dew point: SVP=2219Pa. SVP at 17°C=1940Pa<2219, so dew point is slightly above 17°C (≈19°C where SVP≈2219Pa).

questions.question: Two gases have the same temperature. Gas A has molar mass 4 g/mol (helium) and gas B has molar mass 32 g/mol (oxygen). Compare their mean kinetic energies and rms speeds.
options: Same KE; He has higher rms speed
He has higher KE and higher rms speed
O₂ has higher KE; same rms speed
questions.answer: Same KE; He has higher rms speed
questions.explanation: Mean KE = (3/2)k_BT — depends only on T, not M. At same T, both have same mean KE. But c_rms = √(3RT/M) ∝ 1/√M. He (M=4) has rms speed √(32/4) = √8 = 2.83 times higher than O₂ (M=32).

questions.question: A gas at 1.2×10⁵Pa, 600cm³, 57°C is heated to 177°C and compressed to 300cm³. Find the new pressure.

content.body: When you leave a wet cloth in the sun in Kampala, it dries as water evaporates. When clouds form over Lake Victoria, water vapour condenses. Both processes depend on the concept of saturated vapour pressure — the maximum amount of water vapour the air can hold at a given temperature. This concept is fundamental to meteorology, refrigeration, humidity measurement, and many industrial processes.
Evaporation and Vapour Pressure

**Evaporation:** At any temperature, some molecules near the surface of a liquid have enough KE to overcome the intermolecular bonds and escape into the space above — forming vapour.

**Vapour pressure:** The pressure exerted by the vapour molecules above a liquid surface.

**Unsaturated vapour (USV):** Vapour that is not in equilibrium with its liquid — the evaporation rate exceeds the condensation rate. More liquid could evaporate. Obeys the ideal gas laws approximately.

**Saturated vapour (SV):** Vapour that is in equilibrium with its liquid — the rate of evaporation = rate of condensation. No net change in the amount of vapour.

**Saturated Vapour Pressure (SVP):** The pressure exerted by a saturated vapour. It is the maximum vapour pressure at a given temperature. It depends only on temperature — not on the volume of the space above the liquid.

**Key properties of SVP:**
1. SVP increases with temperature (higher T → more molecules can escape → higher equilibrium pressure)
2. SVP is independent of the volume above the liquid (compressing SV causes condensation until SVP is re-established)
3. SVP is independent of the presence of other gases (Dalton's Law — water SVP is the same in air as in pure water vapour)
4. A liquid boils when its SVP equals the external pressure

**Worked Example 1:** Water boils at 100°C at 1 atm (101,325 Pa). This means the SVP of water at 100°C = 101,325 Pa (atmospheric pressure). At 25°C, SVP of water ≈ 3170 Pa (much lower than atmospheric — water doesn't boil at 25°C).
Variation of SVP with Temperature and P-V Diagrams

**SVP increases with temperature** — following an exponential-like curve described approximately by the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.

**Selected SVP values for water:**
| Temperature (°C) | SVP (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 611 |
| 10 | 1228 |
| 20 | 2338 |
| 25 | 3170 |
| 37 | 6280 |
| 50 | 12,340 |
| 80 | 47,360 |
| 100 | 101,325 |

**P-V diagram for SVP:**

For a fixed amount of substance at constant temperature:
- At large volume: substance is entirely vapour (USV); pressure decreases as V increases (follows Boyle's Law — ideal gas)
- As V decreases: pressure rises until the saturation pressure (SVP) is reached; liquid begins to form
- During condensation (liquid-vapour coexistence): pressure stays constant at SVP as volume continues to decrease
- When all is liquid: pressure rises steeply with further decrease in volume (liquids are nearly incompressible)

**Comparison: USV vs SV:**
| Property | Unsaturated Vapour | Saturated Vapour |
|---|---|---|
| Equilibrium with liquid? | No | Yes |
| Obeys Boyle's Law? | Approximately yes | No (pressure constant during compression) |
| Obeys Charles's Law? | Approximately yes | Only if P is constant |
| Pressure increases with T? | Yes | Yes (SVP increases) |
| Pressure increases with compression? | Yes | No (condensation begins) |
Relative Humidity, Dew Point and Applications

**Relative humidity (RH):** The ratio of the actual vapour pressure in the air to the SVP at the same temperature:

$$RH = \frac{\text{actual vapour pressure}}{\text{SVP at same temperature}} \times 100\%$$

At 100% RH, the air is fully saturated and dew begins to form.

**Dew point:** The temperature to which air must be cooled (at constant pressure and water vapour content) for condensation to begin. When air is cooled below its dew point, water vapour condenses as dew, fog, or clouds.

**Applications in Uganda:**

1. **Weather forecasting:** High RH → high probability of rain. Uganda's tropical climate means RH often exceeds 80% during rainy seasons.

2. **Refrigeration (vapour-compression cycle):** The refrigerant cycles between saturated vapour (in the evaporator) and saturated liquid (in the condenser). At each stage, the substance exists as SVP and its liquid in equilibrium.

3. **Dehumidification:** Air conditioners cool air below its dew point, condensing water vapour. The liquid water drains out. This reduces humidity inside buildings (important in Uganda's humid climate for preventing mould).

4. **Cloud and fog formation:** As warm moist air rises (convection) over Rwenzori or Elgon, it cools adiabatically. When it reaches the dew point, the SVP equals the actual vapour pressure → condensation → clouds form. This explains the perpetual mist around Uganda's mountains.

5. **Crop irrigation and evaporation:** At high RH, evaporation from soil and leaves is slower (less driving force for evaporation). Irrigation is most efficient when RH is low (morning dew point passed, not yet humid afternoon).

6. **Steam engines and turbines:** Steam (water vapour above 100°C at 1 atm) drives turbines. The Karuma hydroelectric dam uses water (not steam), but thermal power stations (like the Namanve plant near Kampala) use steam cycles.

**Worked Example 2:** The relative humidity in Kampala on a morning is 85% at 20°C. The SVP of water at 20°C is 2338 Pa. Find the actual vapour pressure and the mass of water vapour in 1 m³ of air. (M_water = 0.018 kg/mol; R = 8.314 J/mol/K)

Actual vapour pressure = RH × SVP = 0.85 × 2338 = 1987 Pa

Using pV = nRT: n = pV/RT = 1987×1/(8.314×293) = 1987/2436 = 0.815 mol

Mass = n × M = 0.815 × 0.018 = 0.01469 kg/m³ ≈ 14.7 g/m³ of water vapour in the air

content.title: Summary
content.body: Saturated vapour pressure (SVP): maximum vapour pressure at a given temperature; liquid and vapour in equilibrium. SVP increases with temperature; independent of volume and presence of other gases. Boiling occurs when SVP = external pressure. Relative humidity = actual vapour pressure/SVP × 100%. Dew point: temperature at which condensation begins.

content.title: Key Terms
content.body:
• Saturated vapour pressure (SVP): Maximum vapour pressure at a given temperature; vapour in equilibrium with liquid; independent of volume
• Unsaturated vapour: Vapour below the saturation level; not in equilibrium with liquid; approximately obeys ideal gas laws
• Relative humidity: Actual vapour pressure/SVP × 100%; measure of how close the air is to saturation
• Dew point: Temperature at which air must be cooled for condensation to begin; actual vapour pressure = SVP at dew point

questions.question: A real gas deviates most from ideal gas behaviour when:
options: Temperature is high and pressure is low
Temperature is low and pressure is high
Temperature is high and pressure is high
Temperature is low and pressure is low
questions.answer: Temperature is low and pressure is high
questions.explanation: At low temperature, molecules have low KE and intermolecular forces become significant (they can attract each other). At high pressure, molecules are close together so their finite volume and intermolecular interactions matter. Both effects cause maximum deviation from ideal behaviour.

questions.question: Ideal gas: P=1.2×10⁵Pa, V=600cm³, T=57°C. Heated to 177°C and compressed to 300cm³. New pressure?
The pressure of an ideal gas at 300 K is 2.0 × 10⁵ Pa and its density is 1.6 kg/m³. Find the rms speed of its molecules.
State the ideal gas equation. Volume of 3 moles at 47°C and 2.0×10⁵Pa? (R=8.314)
1 mole of ideal gas at STP (0°C, 1.01×10⁵ Pa) occupies:
questions.explanation: V = nRT/P = 1 × 8.314 × 273/(1.01×10⁵) = 2269.7/101,000 = 0.02247 m³ = 22.47 L ≈ 22.4 L. This is the molar volume of any ideal gas at STP.

questions.question: CO₂ (T_c=31°C). At 25°C: is CO₂ a gas or vapour? Can it be liquefied at 25°C? At 35°C?
options: Gas at both; cannot liquefy at either temperature
Vapour at 25°C (T<T_c, can liquefy by pressure); gas at 35°C (T>T_c, cannot liquefy)
Vapour at both; can liquefy at both temperatures
questions.answer: Vapour at 25°C (T<T_c, can liquefy by pressure); gas at 35°C (T>T_c, cannot liquefy)
questions.explanation: Gas: T>T_c (no liquefaction). Vapour: T<T_c (liquefaction by pressure). 25°C<31°C→vapour→can liquefy (CO₂ fire extinguishers). 35°C>31°C→gas→cannot liquefy at any pressure.
